food_name: Pasembur / Indian Rojak (Deep-Fried Mixed Fritters with Sweet-Spicy Potato Sauce)
food_subgroup: Deep-fried mixed fritters with sweet potato sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of Pasembur / Indian Rojak weighs approximately 350g and includes an assortment of deep-fried fritters, bean sprouts, cucumber, and a generous ladle of sweet-spicy potato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pasembur / Indian Rojak — Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pasembur / Indian Rojak (F08010036). All per-100g values are taken as verified from this primary regional source. HPB Foodo is an approved primary source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Deep-Fried Potato and Mixed Fritter Components — University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Pasembur / Indian Rojak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated from the composite of key high-GI components: deep-fried potato (GI ~80–90), wheat batter fritters (GI ~70), and sweet potato sauce (GI ~50–60 for sweet potato). The blended dish GI is moderated by protein from egg and tofu. GL of 39.8 is calculated as (GI 65 × 61.25g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 39.8. This is a referenced analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database methodology.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 415mg per 100g; a standard 350g plate delivers approximately 1,453mg sodium, representing approximately 60% of the 2,400mg daily recommended limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request the sweet potato sauce to be served on the side and use it sparingly, as the sauce is the primary sodium contributor; limit consumption to once per week and avoid adding extra sauce or soy-based condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load — a 350g plate delivers approximately 61.25g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~39.8, driven by deep-fried potato, wheat batter fritters, and the sweet potato sauce containing 5.5g sugar per 100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a plate, request the sauce separately and use sparingly, prioritise the tofu, egg, and fresh vegetable components, and limit consumption to no more than twice per month.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (8.8g fat per 100g) with 1.8g saturated fat and 0.1g trans fat per 100g; deep-frying produces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids that may promote endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to once per fortnight, choose smaller portions, avoid the prawn fritters if managing cholesterol (82mg cholesterol per 100g), and request minimal sauce to reduce sodium and sugar intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying at high temperature generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may promote systemic inflammation; however, the prawn component provides some omega-3 fatty acids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, as the pro-inflammatory AGE load from deep-frying may exacerbate joint inflammation despite the partial omega-3 benefit from prawn fritters.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasembur (known as Indian Rojak in Singapore) is a hearty Indian Muslim hawker dish originating from Penang, Malaysia, and widely popular across Singapore. The dish consists of a colourful assortment of deep-fried fritters — crispy prawn fritters, puffed tofu, fried potato slices, sliced hard-boiled egg, and fried cuttlefish pieces — arranged over a bed of fresh bean sprouts and cucumber slices. The entire platter is then generously dressed with a thick, sweet-spicy potato sauce known as keledek gravy, made from mashed sweet potato, dried chilli, vinegar, and sugar. Crushed peanuts are often scattered on top for added texture. The flavour profile is a complex interplay of savoury, sweet, spicy, and tangy notes, with contrasting textures from the crispy fritters, soft tofu, crunchy bean sprouts, and smooth sauce. Nutritionally, a standard 350g plate provides approximately 578 kcal, 26.25g protein, 30.8g fat, and 61.25g carbohydrate, with a high sodium load of approximately 1,453mg. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains multiple allergens including crustaceans, egg, gluten, peanuts, soy, and molluscs. Despite sharing the name 'rojak' with the Malay fruit-vegetable rojak, Pasembur / Indian Rojak is a completely distinct preparation. It is a high-GI, high-GL dish unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: Indian rojak vs Malay rojak difference

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (an approved primary regional source) with verified confidence 1.0 for all per-100g fields. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, which slightly reduces overall confidence from perfect; however, the nutrient data itself is exact-match from an approved source, justifying high confidence overall.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of April 2026. trans_fat_g value of 0.1g per 100g is sourced from HPB Foodo verified data. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database component values for deep-fried potato, wheat batter fritters, and sweet potato; no direct GI entry exists for Pasembur / Indian Rojak. Glycemic load of 39.8 calculated as (65 × 61.25) / 100 for a 350g serving. The dish description in the input incorrectly states 'sweet-spicy peanut sauce' — the sauce is a sweet potato (keledek) sauce; peanuts are a topping, not the sauce base. This has been corrected in the output. Hindi local name 'इंडियन रोजाक' is a transliteration as no established native Hindi culinary term exists for this dish.
